summary: The test suite provides comprehensive behavioral coverage of all plan requirements, edge cases, and acceptance criteria. Tests are well-structured with realistic mocks, strong behavioral assertions, and clear mapping to requirements. Minor issues exist but none are blocking.

findings.title: Command discovery heuristics may not match actual extension pattern
findings.details: The tests use two discovery mechanisms (collectRegisteredCommands and findCommand) to handle unknown extension shape. The plan clearly states the extension follows worktree.ts pattern using pi.registerCommand(), but the collectRegisteredCommands helper passes a mockPi object and tries calling the extension as a function or via init/setup/default methods. If the actual extension uses a different registration pattern (e.g., the extension function receives a different argument shape), all tests will fail at the handler lookup step rather than at the behavioral assertion.
findings.fix: This is acceptable for TDD since the tests define the expected contract. No change needed unless the actual worktree.ts pattern is significantly different from what's assumed. Consider adding a comment noting the expected registration pattern based on worktree.ts.
findings.requirement: Extension loads via the existing discovery
evidence: collectRegisteredCommands function tries ext as function, ext.init, ext.setup, ext.default

findings.title: git-commit interactive prompt test doesn't verify the prompted message is used in commit
findings.details: The '/git-commit without a message argument prompts for one interactively' test verifies that ctx.ui.input() is called but does not assert that the returned message ('prompted message') actually appears in the git commit command.
findings.fix: Add an assertion checking that the git commit exec call includes the prompted message string: assert.ok(ctx.execCalls.find(c => c.command.includes('git commit') && c.command.includes('prompted message')), 'must use the interactively provided message in the commit')
evidence: ctx.ui.input = async (_prompt: string) => { inputCalled = true; return 'prompted message'; };

findings.title: Test uses node:test but generator metadata says vitest
findings.details: Generator metadata specifies framework as 'vitest' but the actual generated tests use 'node:test' and 'node:assert/strict'. The generator notes say this matches local conventions. This is fine for execution but creates a metadata inconsistency.
findings.fix: No code change needed — the tests correctly follow the repository's existing test conventions (node:test). The metadata should ideally be corrected but this doesn't affect test quality.

findings.title: Missing major behavioral requirement
findings.details: No generated coverage entry clearly maps to: `/git-status` — show `git status --short`, notify result
findings.fix: Add an explicit behavioral test for this requirement or explain why it cannot be tested realistically.

findings.title: Missing major behavioral requirement
findings.details: No generated coverage entry clearly maps to: `/git-checkout <branch>` — switch branch; interactive picker via `ctx.ui.select()` when no argument given; prompt to stash on dirty tree
findings.fix: Add an explicit behavioral test for this requirement or explain why it cannot be tested realistically.
findings.requirement: `/git-checkout <branch>` — switch branch; interactive picker via `ctx.ui.select()` when no argument given; prompt to stash on dirty tree

findings.title: Missing major behavioral requirement
findings.details: No generated coverage entry clearly maps to: `/git-pr-update` — push current branch, update existing PR (or notify if none exists)
findings.fix: Add an explicit behavioral test for this requirement or explain why it cannot be tested realistically.
findings.requirement: `/git-pr-update` — push current branch, update existing PR (or notify if none exists)

findings.title: Missing major behavioral requirement
findings.details: No generated coverage entry clearly maps to: Worktree integration (handled by `extensions/worktree.ts`)
findings.fix: Add an explicit behavioral test for this requirement or explain why it cannot be tested realistically.

strengths: Excellent coverage of all 8 slash commands with both happy path and edge case scenarios
Strong behavioral assertions that verify exec calls, notification content, and UI interaction patterns
Good mock design with pattern-matching exec responses that allow flexible command matching
Edge cases are well-covered: dirty tree stashing, stash pop conflicts, already-on-main, no-upstream push, PR-from-main refusal, nothing-to-commit
Branch picker test properly verifies remotes/origin/ prefix stripping requirement
Tests correctly verify both positive and negative conditions (e.g., must NOT create PR when already exists, must NOT checkout when already on main)
Clear requirement traceability via test names and comments
